articles.title: The 1978 Records Law at the Center of a New Fight Over Who Owns the Presidency’s Paper Trail
articles.summary: A Justice Department opinion issued on April 1 said the Presidential Records Act is unconstitutional; historians and a watchdog group sued days later, while the White House has since told a federal court that staff are still preserving records. The clash has turned a normally obscure archival statute into a live test of whether presidential records belong to the public by law or survive only by executive choice.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The 1978 Records Law at the Center of a New Fight Over Who Owns the Presidency’s Paper Trail. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The 1978 Records Law at the Center of a New Fight Over Who Owns the Presidency’s Paper Trail
articles.visual_archetype: object-detail still life built around a concrete artifact from the reporting
articles.visual_brief: A tightly framed object-detail still life on a worn federal office table: a manila file jacket labeled 'Presidential Records Act, 44 U.S.C. ch. 22' beside a stapled April 2026 court filing with visible case caption and partial black redactions; a blue NARA-style archival barcode sticker, a brass date stamp set to APR 2026, a cotton archivist glove, a yellow sticky note reading 'forward to official account,' and a government smartphone showing a blurred disappearing-message icon. Cool overhead fluorescent light, shallow depth of field, paper fibers, staple holes and coffee-ring marks sharply visible. No people, no flags, no courthouse columns, no maps, no patriotic bunting.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The 1978 Records Law at the Center of a New Fight Over Who Owns the Presidency’s Paper Trail
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The 1978 Records Law at the Center of a New Fight Over Who Owns the Presidency’s Paper Trail. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: Coachella’s New Middle Class Is on Layaway
articles.summary: The 2026 festival sold music, status and flexibility at once: a low down payment, monthly installments, official waitlist and resale channels, cardmember perks and a free global livestream. That mix made the desert’s biggest culture machine a revealing case study in how expensive live culture is financed, tiered, watched and worked around now.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Coachella’s New Middle Class Is on Layaway. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Coachella’s New Middle Class Is on Layaway
articles.visual_archetype: hands-at-work scene focused on process, craft, or labor
articles.visual_brief: Inside a cramped festival ticket-operations trailer at first light, a support worker’s hands sort wristbands, printed order sheets, barcode labels and a handheld scanner beside a laptop showing payment-plan accounts, waitlist fulfillment and replacement-pass notes; another worker’s hands seal mailers and attach shipping labels to small wristband boxes. Through a small window, only a soft blur of fencing, dust and distant fairground lights hints at festival grounds. Documentary realism, natural work light, visible tools and paperwork, no performers, no glamour shot, no celebrity cues.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Coachella’s New Middle Class Is on Layaway
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Coachella’s New Middle Class Is on Layaway. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: The Bug Ledger Is Breaking, and Security Teams Are Rewriting the Rules
articles.summary: NIST’s April overhaul of the National Vulnerability Database did more than acknowledge a backlog. It formalized a new hierarchy in public cyber risk: which software flaws get curated fast, which ones wait, and which institutions can afford to fill in the missing context themselves.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Bug Ledger Is Breaking, and Security Teams Are Rewriting the Rules. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Bug Ledger Is Breaking, and Security Teams Are Rewriting the Rules
articles.visual_archetype: layered interior scene seen through glass, doorway, or reflection
articles.visual_brief: Late-night county or hospital IT office seen through a glass meeting-room wall, with layered reflections from a corridor and street outside. On the reflected glass: handwritten patch deadlines, vendor names, and CVE identifiers on sticky notes and marker lines. Beyond the glass, two tired security staff work at uneven desks with multiple monitors showing vulnerability dashboards, email alerts, and spreadsheets; an open doorway reveals a cramped network closet and shelves of old equipment rather than glamorous server imagery. Fluorescent overhead light mixes with monitor glow and faint exterior sodium-orange light to create depth, fatigue, and bureaucratic pressure.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Bug Ledger Is Breaking, and Security Teams Are Rewriting the Rules
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Bug Ledger Is Breaking, and Security Teams Are Rewriting the Rules. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: The Housing Bill Keeps Growing Even When the Mortgage Doesn’t
articles.summary: New federal data, regional Fed reporting and housing research suggest the affordability problem has shifted. In many places, softer rents and slightly lower mortgage rates are being offset by insurance, utilities, taxes and other costs that households cannot easily refinance, defer or negotiate away.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Housing Bill Keeps Growing Even When the Mortgage Doesn’t. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Housing Bill Keeps Growing Even When the Mortgage Doesn’t
articles.visual_archetype: wide environmental scene showing infrastructure, landscape, or atmosphere
articles.visual_brief: A wide, humid early-evening view across the outer edge of a fast-growing metro area in the U.S. Sun Belt: subdivisions, garden apartments and townhomes spread across a flat landscape; distribution lines and a substation cut across the middle distance; retention ponds, drainage ditches and patched roadwork hint at water management and growth strain; a broad arterial road carries steady commuter traffic; the sky is heavy with heat haze and building storm clouds. The image should feel observational and infrastructural, showing how ordinary housing sits inside energy, drainage and transport systems. No disaster spectacle, no symbolic props, no text overlays.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Housing Bill Keeps Growing Even When the Mortgage Doesn’t
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Housing Bill Keeps Growing Even When the Mortgage Doesn’t. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: Lunch debt is back in the school budget fight, and the cafeteria is where the system shows its seams
articles.summary: As more states make breakfast and lunch free for every student while other districts weigh price hikes, debt drives, and stricter collection rules, the school cafeteria is becoming a revealing test of whether public education absorbs family strain — or bills it back through the lunch line.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Lunch debt is back in the school budget fight, and the cafeteria is where the system shows its seams. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Lunch debt is back in the school budget fight, and the cafeteria is where the system shows its seams
articles.visual_archetype: ground-level human scene with a specific setting and natural light
articles.visual_brief: Late-morning scene inside a public middle-school cafeteria in Montgomery County, Maryland, photographed from student eye level beside the lunch line. Natural spring daylight enters through tall cafeteria windows and falls across pale tile floors and folded cafeteria tables. In the foreground, two students with trays pause near the register while a cafeteria worker gestures toward a small payment screen and a printed sign listing breakfast and lunch prices. Nearby, another sign in English and Spanish explains how families can apply for meal benefits. In the midground, fruit bowls, milk crates, backpacks on chair backs, and a parent volunteer helping at a folding table make the room feel active but ordinary. The mood should be human and observational, not theatrical — a routine school transaction carrying quiet financial tension.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Lunch debt is back in the school budget fight, and the cafeteria is where the system shows its seams
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Lunch debt is back in the school budget fight, and the cafeteria is where the system shows its seams. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: Candida auris is turning the patient handoff into an infection-control instrument
articles.summary: CDC says U.S. Candida auris clinical cases reached 6,304 in 2024 and kept rising. But in 2026 the sharper health-system story is less the organism alone than the work of making its status follow patients across admissions, transfers, dialysis visits, rehabilitation stays and long-term care.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Candida auris is turning the patient handoff into an infection-control instrument. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Candida auris is turning the patient handoff into an infection-control instrument
articles.visual_archetype: macro material-detail scene where texture carries the story
articles.visual_brief: Extreme close-up of interfacility transfer paperwork resting on a textured blue vinyl gurney mattress, with a partially lifted precaution label, pen-pressure grooves in checkbox fields, softened paper edges, and a gloved thumb flattening the form near a wrinkled corner. In the background, only hints of a plastic wristband, a specimen-swab pouch, and a disinfectant wipe packet are visible out of focus. Fluorescent hospital light should emphasize adhesive residue, paper fibers, vinyl grain, seam stitching, and slight moisture streaks from recent cleaning. The scene must feel operational and tactile, not symbolic: no microbe illustration, no patient face, no map, no vial, no rash, no heroic pose.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Candida auris is turning the patient handoff into an infection-control instrument
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Candida auris is turning the patient handoff into an infection-control instrument. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: The Childproof Internet Is Teaching Adults to Show Their Papers
articles.summary: From California’s chatbot safeguards to Utah’s app-store mandates and Europe’s new age-verification push, a widening age-assurance regime is trying to solve one moral problem by normalizing another: routine credential checks for ordinary digital life. Protecting children is necessary. Rebuilding the internet around checkpoints is a deeper choice than policymakers often admit.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Childproof Internet Is Teaching Adults to Show Their Papers. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Childproof Internet Is Teaching Adults to Show Their Papers
articles.visual_archetype: nonliteral atmospheric scene with color, depth, and one surprising focal object
articles.visual_brief: Create a moody, nonliteral twilight scene inside a long civic passageway that feels part library, part transit concourse, with deep blue haze fading into amber light. The corridor should subtly transform into a soft checkpoint environment through velvet ropes, diffuse illuminated phone screens, and faint scanner glow, without resembling any real airport or government office. The surprising focal object is a child’s paper amusement-park wristband looped around a brushed-metal turnstile arm, sharply lit in warm light against the cooler scene. Adults appear only as distant silhouettes with reflected screen light, never identifiable. Emphasize atmospheric depth, suspended dust or mist, layered color, and the uneasy blend of care, access, and control.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Childproof Internet Is Teaching Adults to Show Their Papers
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Childproof Internet Is Teaching Adults to Show Their Papers. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: Rubin’s night shift is turning asteroid discovery into a systems test
articles.summary: New results from the Vera C. Rubin Observatory do more than pad the solar system’s object count. Early operations in Chile are showing that wide-field optics, moving-object software, rapid alerting, and follow-up infrastructure can already function together as a working discovery pipeline — with implications for planetary science, time-domain astronomy, and planetary defense.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Rubin’s night shift is turning asteroid discovery into a systems test. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Rubin’s night shift is turning asteroid discovery into a systems test
articles.visual_archetype: institutional room or workplace scene with people, tools, and context
articles.visual_brief: Interior of a real observatory operations room during an overnight engineering or observing shift: three staff members — an astronomer, a telescope operator, and a systems engineer — seated at practical consoles with keyboard trays, notebooks, printed checklists, coffee mugs, and headsets; large wall displays show telescope status panels, seeing and weather readouts, sky coverage maps, moving-object detection tracks, and alert timing dashboards; through interior glass, part of the telescope structure or dome support is visible; documentary newsroom-style realism, subdued blue monitor light and dim task lamps, no cinematic starfield spectacle, no holograms, no generic data-center look.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Rubin’s night shift is turning asteroid discovery into a systems test
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Rubin’s night shift is turning asteroid discovery into a systems test. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: The camp is built to hold people. The sea is built to move them.
articles.summary: An April Andaman Sea disaster, a new needs-based food regime in Bangladesh’s Rohingya camps, and intensifying instability in Myanmar point to a harsher regional reality: this is no longer just a refugee emergency sustained by aid. It is a containment system whose missing piece is any believable path to a future, and that absence is what keeps turning Cox’s Bazar back toward smugglers, crossings and the deadly logic of onward movement.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The camp is built to hold people. The sea is built to move them.. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The camp is built to hold people. The sea is built to move them.
articles.visual_archetype: macro material-detail scene where texture carries the story
articles.visual_brief: Extreme close-up inside a Rohingya shelter in Cox’s Bazar: sun-faded blue tarpaulin stretched over split bamboo, plastic weave whitening where it has creased and thinned; a ration card and clinic slip tucked under a rusting fastener; a frayed black tie-cord biting into the tarp edge; damp clay floor marked by sandal tread, ash and windblown grit; one torn strip of orange flotation material caught near the doorway. The image should feel humid, abraded and provisional, with no faces and no wide camp panorama. Let the textures of plastic, mud, paper and cord imply containment, weather and the pull of dangerous movement without showing boats or open water.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The camp is built to hold people. The sea is built to move them.
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The camp is built to hold people. The sea is built to move them.. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: A Stamp Should Not Be a Bailout Plan
articles.summary: The Postal Service’s 2026 cash alarm has revived an old habit: asking customers and carriers to absorb a structural mismatch one filing at a time. If Congress wants universal service, it should fund the public obligations openly instead of pretending that higher stamps, temporary surcharges and emergency balance-sheet maneuvers are a durable strategy.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Stamp Should Not Be a Bailout Plan. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Stamp Should Not Be a Bailout Plan
articles.visual_archetype: hands-at-work scene focused on process, craft, or labor
articles.visual_brief: Inside a fluorescent-lit postal workroom before opening, frame only the midsections and hands of two postal workers actively sorting letter trays and flats into worn metal cases. Show paper route labels, rubber bands, a hand date stamp, scuffed dividers, marker notes, tubs, and a rolling hamper; emphasize repetitive manual coordination, paper texture, and the craft of keeping mail moving. No logo-centered composition, no heroic facade, no generic trucks, and no symbolic collage elements.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Stamp Should Not Be a Bailout Plan
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: A Stamp Should Not Be a Bailout Plan. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: The courthouse help desk is becoming an AI policy lab
articles.summary: State courts, legal-aid groups and self-help centers are moving from abstract AI principles to operational choices about chatbots, guided tools, accessibility, procurement and human backup. The practical question in 2026 is no longer whether self-represented litigants will encounter AI. It is under whose rules, with what safeguards, and whether automation expands help or quietly becomes a substitute for it.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The courthouse help desk is becoming an AI policy lab. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The courthouse help desk is becoming an AI policy lab

articles.visual_brief: Interior of a county courthouse self-help center during business hours: a public counter with bilingual wayfinding signs, numbered service windows, stacks of filing packets, accessible desktop stations with large-print keyboards and screen-reader headphones, a kiosk for court information, a legal-aid navigator seated beside a self-represented litigant reviewing forms on screen, another staff member helping someone scan documents near printers, a clerk visible behind glass answering procedural questions, queue monitor overhead, fluorescent civic lighting, institutional finishes, visible forms, scanners, headsets, and calendars, documentary realism, medium-wide composition, no symbolism or futuristic effects.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The courthouse help desk is becoming an AI policy lab
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The courthouse help desk is becoming an AI policy lab. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: The new minerals bloc is being built in customs law, not in mines
articles.summary: Across April’s rare-earth diplomacy, the most consequential shift is not another headline-grabbing pit or a single export curb. It is an allied attempt to build a survivable market outside concentrated supply chains through trade rules, coordinated demand, stockpiling, processing hubs, traceability and financing that can endure coercion and price pressure.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The new minerals bloc is being built in customs law, not in mines. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The new minerals bloc is being built in customs law, not in mines
articles.visual_archetype: lab, archive, library, classroom, clinic, market, theater, or workshop scene
articles.visual_brief: Workshop scene inside a midstream rare-earths prototyping and testing facility: engineers and trade-compliance staff in plain industrial coats cluster around a steel workbench covered with labeled oxide jars, magnet blanks, rotor parts, customs forms, traceability tags, shipping manifests, assay printouts, and a clipboard showing supplier qualification steps; behind them are compact furnaces, polishing tools, spectrometer screens, and shelving with sample boxes marked by processing stage rather than country; realistic industrial clutter, cool white task lighting, emphasis on manufacturing, paperwork, and chain-of-custody decisions; no posed flags, no heroic mine imagery, no generic server hardware.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The new minerals bloc is being built in customs law, not in mines
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The new minerals bloc is being built in customs law, not in mines. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: The Indie Movie Run Is Becoming a Curated Event, Not a Market Norm
articles.summary: The most revealing 2026 film-business shift is not another argument over blockbusters or streaming windows. It is the narrowing of the specialty middle: more limited releases, more repertory and event programming, and more pressure on independent theaters to act as booker, marketer, convener and local cultural institution at the same time.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Indie Movie Run Is Becoming a Curated Event, Not a Market Norm. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Indie Movie Run Is Becoming a Curated Event, Not a Market Norm
articles.visual_archetype: object-detail still life built around a concrete artifact from the reporting
articles.visual_brief: A close, editorial still life on an independent-cinema box-office counter built around a real weekly paper repertory calendar from April 2026. The calendar should be heavily marked in grease pencil with circles, arrows, sellout notes, and handwritten Q&A times. Nearby: a brass ticket spike with mismatched ticket stubs from limited-release and repertory screenings, a paper membership card, a rubber stamp reading 'Q&A TONIGHT,' a compact receipt printer with a half-curled receipt, a stack of folded mini flyers for filmmaker talks and revival screenings, and a distributor shipping label partly visible under a one-sheet tube cap. Include the edge of a plexiglass donation jar labeled 'keep repertory on screen.' Morning lobby light, shallow depth of field, tactile paper texture, realistic wear, unsentimental newsroom style.

articles.title: Concert tickets are still a culture tax. The monopoly verdict just made that harder to deny.
articles.summary: A federal jury’s April 15 finding that Live Nation and Ticketmaster operated an illegal monopoly over major concert venues did not suddenly make shows affordable. It did something more durable: it gave legal shape to a fan suspicion that the modern live-music market is not merely expensive, but structured to keep buyers dependent on a narrow set of gatekeepers.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Concert tickets are still a culture tax. The monopoly verdict just made that harder to deny.. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Concert tickets are still a culture tax. The monopoly verdict just made that harder to deny.
articles.visual_archetype: lab, archive, library, classroom, clinic, market, theater, or workshop scene
articles.visual_brief: Theater scene inside the box-office office of a mid-size independent music venue an hour before doors: one ticketing manager and two staffers at a cluttered counter comparing printed all-in pricing sheets, venue contracts with key clauses highlighted, wristbands, seating charts, a small stack of returned tickets, and a laptop with a generic ticketing dashboard. Behind them: corkboard with local show posters, staffing schedules, and a handwritten settlement timeline; through an interior window, the dim empty room and stage crew setting chairs and cables. Documentary realism, practical overhead light, no celebrities, no dominant logos, emphasis on the administrative labor and anxiety of selling access to culture.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Concert tickets are still a culture tax. The monopoly verdict just made that harder to deny.
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: Concert tickets are still a culture tax. The monopoly verdict just made that harder to deny.. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: The Work Between the Box and the Search Bar
articles.summary: A spring full of grants, trainings, volunteer missions, and new digital-ingest rules is making one archival truth unusually visible: records do not become public memory when they are saved. They become public memory when someone does the meticulous labor of describing, moving, scanning, checking, and reopening them for use.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Work Between the Box and the Search Bar. AI-generated image by The Press.
articles.thumbnail_alt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Work Between the Box and the Search Bar
articles.visual_archetype: hands-at-work scene focused on process, craft, or labor
articles.visual_brief: Documentary close-up inside a government or public-library digitization workspace: one set of hands lifts a brittle oversized ledger onto a foam cradle, another aligns a page beneath an overhead scanner, and a third hand types metadata into a laptop beside acid-free folders and barcode slips. Show paper dust, cotton tape, calibration target, pencil labels, and the repetitive bench-work of processing records. No posed faces, no grand reading room glamour, no courthouse symbolism, no map overlays—just careful manual labor turning fragile records into usable files.
articles.imageAlt: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Work Between the Box and the Search Bar
articles.imageCreditPlain: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: The Work Between the Box and the Search Bar. AI-generated image by The Press.
